title: FreeBSD14.2 Podman 5.2.5_2 error on push to docker hub: error: writing blob: patch .. happened during read: digest did not match, expected ..
body:
### Issue Description

Podman 5.2.5 on FreeBSD 14.2, FreeBSD updated to 9feb2025, running as root:

root@freebsd:~ # podman images
REPOSITORY                      TAG         IMAGE ID      CREATED         SIZE
docker.io/dragos35/freebsd14.2  latest      627ca8838133  38 minutes ago  514 MB
localhost/freebsd               14.2        627ca8838133  38 minutes ago  514 MB

root@freebsd:~ # podman push docker.io/dragos35/freebsd14.2:latest
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob ea5ddf9d8bc9 done   |
Error: writing blob: Patch "https://registry-1.docker.io/v2/dragos35/freebsd14.2/blobs/uploads/220d7490-b231-4225-869e-3edb7004397c?_state=PnPhPReIIABiY967HdPdWf9GumOHh-tVhMWovIRiOSx7Ik5hbWUiOiJkcmFnb3MzNS9mcmVlYnNkMTQuMiIsIlVVSUQiOiIyMjBkNzQ5MC1iMjMxLTQyMjUtODY5ZS0zZWRiNzAwNDM5N2MiLCJPZmZzZXQiOjAsIlN0YXJ0ZWRBdCI6IjIwMjUtMDItMDlUMDY6MzU6NDEuMjIyNjU0OTM5WiJ9": happened during read: Digest did not match, expected sha256:ea5ddf9d8bc93e31d35d234af37f14773cd78efdca835c6303de2556966e12e8, got sha256:01ae2daa9fbc8a7d8c34942393c8717273c20bcbc62455861c3d4de476e21bab

root@freebsd:~ # df -h | grep fdesc
fdescfs               1.0K      0B    1.0K     0%    /dev/fd

Please let me know if you need more info to debug this.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. freebsd-update fetch
2. freebsd-update install
3. pkg install podman
4. mount -t cd9660 /dev/md0 /mnt
5. cp /usr/local/etc/containers/pf.conf.sample /etc/pf.conf
6. vim /etc/pf.conf and replaced with my network card name
7. fetch https://download.freebsd.org/releases/amd64/amd64/ISO-IMAGES/14.2/FreeBSD-14.2-RELEASE-amd64-memstick.img
8. mdconfig -a -t vnode -f FreeBSD-14.2-RELEASE-amd64-memstick.img -u 07. mkdir freebsd-rootfs8. tar -xJf /mnt/usr/freebsd-dist/base.txz -C freebsd-rootfs9. podman import freebsd-rootfs.tar freebsd:minimal
10. (podman images)
11. podman run -it freebsd:minimal /bin/sh0 # exit form container # we see its running well
12. podman commit $(podman ps -lq) my-freebsd:latest
13.  podman tag localhost/my-freebsd:latest docker.io/dragos35/freebsd14.2:latest
14. podman push docker.io/dragos35/freebsd14.2:latest
15. error

### Describe the results you received

Looks like SHA hash differences in some blob. 

root@freebsd:~ # podman push docker.io/dragos35/freebsd14.2:latest
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob ea5ddf9d8bc9 done   |
Error: writing blob: Patch "https://registry-1.docker.io/v2/dragos35/freebsd14.2/blobs/uploads/220d7490-b231-4225-869e-3edb7004397c?_state=PnPhPReIIABiY967HdPdWf9GumOHh-tVhMWovIRiOSx7Ik5hbWUiOiJkcmFnb3MzNS9mcmVlYnNkMTQuMiIsIlVVSUQiOiIyMjBkNzQ5MC1iMjMxLTQyMjUtODY5ZS0zZWRiNzAwNDM5N2MiLCJPZmZzZXQiOjAsIlN0YXJ0ZWRBdCI6IjIwMjUtMDItMDlUMDY6MzU6NDEuMjIyNjU0OTM5WiJ9": happened during read: Digest did not match, expected sha256:ea5ddf9d8bc93e31d35d234af37f14773cd78efdca835c6303de2556966e12e8, got sha256:01ae2daa9fbc8a7d8c34942393c8717273c20bcbc62455861c3d4de476e21bab

### Describe the results you expected

I expected push to docker.io to complete successfully.

### podman info output

```yaml

root@freebsd:~ # pkg info podman
podman-5.2.5_2
Name           : podman
Version        : 5.2.5_2
Installed on   : Sun Feb  9 07:44:28 2025 EET
Origin         : sysutils/podman
Architecture   : FreeBSD:14:amd64
Prefix         : /usr/local
Categories     : sysutils
Licenses       : APACHE20
Maintainer     : dfr@FreeBSD.org
WWW            : https://podman.io/
Comment        : Manage Pods, Containers and Container Images
Shared Libs required:
        libgpgme.so.11
Annotations    :
        FreeBSD_version: 1401000
        build_timestamp: 2025-01-30T05:54:04+0000
        built_by       : poudriere-git-3.4.2
        port_checkout_unclean: no
        port_git_hash  : dddb9bab3
        ports_top_checkout_unclean: no
        ports_top_git_hash: 2e0e0c62c
        repo_type      : binary
        repository     : FreeBSD
Flat size      : 102MiB
Description    :
Podman (Pod Manager) is a fully featured container engine that is a
simple daemonless tool.  Podman provides a Docker-CLI comparable
command line that eases the transition from other container engines
and allows the management of pods, containers and images.  Simply put:
alias docker=podman.

Podman uses Buildah(1) internally to create container images.  Both
tools share image (not container) storage, hence each can use or
manipulate images (but not containers) created by the other.
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

None, its just a default FreeBSD 14.2 updated to 9feb2025.

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body: just a small title change
Please try with the latest podman version

@Luap99 found this via our IRC chat, I'm seeing the same issue while creating
multi-arch manifests for our FreeBSD release process.

further digging shows its related to the `minimal` image type only, on either architecture.

```

I don't have newer podman here but this should allow reproducing it.

```
$ curl -#o - https://people.freebsd.org/~dch/pub/oci/20250410.tar.xz | tar xf -
$ podman import 15-CURRENT/amd64/container-image-minimal.txz
$ podman import 15-CURRENT/arm64/container-image-minimal.txz
$ podman manifest create localhost:5000/freebsd-minimal:15.snap20250410100823
$ podman manifest add --arch arm64 localhost:5000/freebsd-minimal:15.snap20250410100823 localhost/freebsd-minimal:15.0-CURRENT-amd64
$ podman manifest add --arch amd64 localhost:5000/freebsd-minimal:15.snap20250410100823 localhost/freebsd-minimal:15.0-CURRENT-aarch64
successfully created manifest: localhost:5000/freebsd-minimal:15.snap20250410100823

$ podman tag localhost:5000/freebsd-minimal:15.snap20250410100823 localhost:5000/freebsd-minimal:15.snap
applied tag: localhost:5000/freebsd-minimal:15.snap

$ podman manifest push --log-level=debug --tls-verify=false localhost:5000/freebsd-minimal:15.snap20250410100823 localhost:5000/freebsd-minimal:15.snap20250410100823
💩

You need podman 5.4.2 if you want to import xz compressed images https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/25593

You could work around it by decompressing on the host first before importing.

This doesn’t make sense to me — 7/9 images produced the same way on the same day *do* work, only 2 others don’t. Is this just magical luck?

Anyway thanks for the clarification I will see if we can get the port updated and try that.
I looked into this - I think that with some of the images, bsdtar generates slightly different tar files to the go tar library, possibly due to bsdtar's handling of sparse files.

> You need podman 5.4.2 if you want to import xz compressed images [#25593](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/25593)
> 
> You could work around it by decompressing on the host first before importing.

Podman 5.4 does import the xz compressed images just fine but this is not the problem. The issue is that when we try to push from local storage to some public registry, the code tries to verify the diff-id of the single layer in the image and comes up with a different hash. This seems to be an inconsistency between the bsdtar generated tar file for the image layer and the one reconstituted from local storage and tar metadata stashed on import by the tar-split library.

When I diffed hex dumps of the two tar files, it looked like bsdtar was using a sparse file extension at the point where the files diverged but I wasn't able to work around it by disabling sparse file handling when the image was generated. It seems like tar-split doesn't like bsdtar :(

Note: containerd does not have any problems pushing this image, likely because it keeps the original tar files that make up the image instead of regenerating them from the unpacked local storage copy.
We may be able to work around this by using gnutar instead of bsdtar in the shell scripts that generate these images. I'll try that over the weekend.

> We may be able to work around this by using gnutar instead of bsdtar in the shell scripts that generate these images. I'll try that over the weekend.

I can confirm that if I use gtar 1.35 to create the tarball for the problematic image, I can successfully load, save and push. This unblocks things for us but this is still a bug and adds another dependency to the FreeBSD build which isn't ideal. I would like to keep this issue open to track the incompatibility between bsdtar and Podman.
I wanted to attach an example oci-archive image which shows the problem but github isn't letting me attach tar files for some reason.

https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/writing-on-github/working-with-advanced-formatting/attaching-files

They are very restrictive, you could gzip compress it.

---

@mtrmac Would be the best person to comment on the compression details here.

> This seems to be an inconsistency between the bsdtar generated tar file for the image layer and the one reconstituted from local storage and tar metadata stashed on import by the tar-split library.

That’s a good hypothesis. `tar-split`’s whole purpose is to recreate exactly the original uncompressed version — but I wouldn’t be too surprised if there were obscure unhandled cases.

I have tried
```console
% xzcat 15-CURRENT/arm64/container-image-minimal.txz > original.tar
% ../../../vbatts/tar-split/tar-split d ./original.tar > split-output               
INFO[0000] created tar-data.json.gz from ./original.tar (read 10714112 bytes) 
mitr@mitr-mac t % rm -rf t
mitr@mitr-mac t % mkdir t 
mitr@mitr-mac t % cd t
mitr@mitr-mac t % tar xf ../original.tar
mitr@mitr-mac t % cd ..
mitr@mitr-mac t % ../../../vbatts/tar-split/tar-split a --input tar-data.json.gz --path $(pwd)/t  > recreated.tar
INFO[0000] created - from /Users/mitr/Go/src/github.com/containers/skopeo/t/t and tar-data.json.gz (wrote 10714112 bytes) 
mitr@mitr-mac t % cmp original.tar recreated.tar                                                                 
```
(on a macOS, i.e. the macOS `tar` implementation extracting the data), and that did not show any difference, neither for arm64 nor amd64.

---

(If the tar-split investigation failed, I’d be somewhat interested in seeing whether this reproduces with a `vfs` graph driver, to rule out something specific to `zfs` which we don’t look at much. (I don’t _expect_ this to make a difference.))
The problem tar file is not the outer tar (the one containing index.json, oci-layout and blobs) but in the gzipped tar blob for the image layer which in this case will be the largest blob file.

Thanks, I am confused how the `podman import` reproducer ends up extracting the OCI structure inside `container-image-minimal`.

---

Anyway, the reproducer above does reproduce this with `15-CURRENT/arm64/container-image-minimal.txz`’s `7c461561b66800c67951bc27de300c3ce05f97ab27151b22f8fb2b44a02c6104`:
```
29691904  original.tar
31919104  recreated.tar
```
The first difference is `/var/db/services.db`, which is a sparse file (`GNU.sparse.major=1` `GNU.sparse.minor=0`.

---

Actually, the tar-split format _does not support sparse files at all_: the metadata format simply cannot represent that.

Compare https://github.com/opencontainers/image-spec/blob/main/layer.md#file-attributes .

---

Now… should that be considered an invalid input, or an implementation bug in `podman import`? Just going by the documentation, “implementation bug” seems natural, but fixing that would be expensive (the code would have to parse the whole file — probably not that bad — and create a new version of the tar without the sparse feature — possibly rather expensive, especially if the tar file were created for the single purpose of running `podman import`).

In my testing, I also noticed that the divergence was at services.db and that it was in some kind of sparse format. I tried to disable sparse with bsdtar but there was still some kind of difference, although it is quite possible that my attempt to disable sparse didn't work properly.

If the sparse file could be detected on import, I think it would be entirely reasonable to generate an error when attempting the load the oci-archive. I'm not sure how hard that would be though.

It seems clear that tar is not the best choice of archive format here - perhaps some future version of the image format could avoid using tar altogether. Tar format is weakly specificied and as we see here, there is divergence between implementations which makes it a poor foundation for the image spec. In the meantime, we can use GNU tar or perhaps find a workaround for bsdtar.

> If the sparse file could be detected on import, I think it would be entirely reasonable to generate an error when attempting the load the oci-archive. I'm not sure how hard that would be though.

The code is already computing a digest of the file, so it should not be too expensive. It’s annoying that https://pkg.go.dev/archive/tar does not expose a stable API to determine whether a header is sparse, but I suppose we could hard-code the situations recognized by the current reader.
 
> It seems clear that tar is not the best choice of archive format here - perhaps some future version of the image format could avoid using tar altogether.

I mean, yes, and there have been _many_ ideas floating around over the years, but ultimately that’s not directly relevant, other than that `podman import` currently makes the optimization of using the original tar as is. If we went with a different format, we would have to _always_ convert the format on `podman import`; and converting the format from tar to expected-form-of-tar is something we have the option of doing today.

I'm not sure podman import should convert on import - surely that would change the image ID since the layer's diff-id would change. I wasn't thinking that podman should change to use a different format, just hoping that some future version of the image specification could deprecate using tar in this way :).

AFAIK `podman import` only accepts a tarball as input, and creates an entirely new (empty and ~unusable) config. Is that not so?

The image ID comes from the config digest, and nothing promises that `podman import` will continue generate a config with predictable digests = the image ID of the outcome of `podman import` is unspecified and could, in principle, change from run to run.

> AFAIK `podman import` only accepts a tarball as input, and creates an entirely new (empty and ~unusable) config. Is that not so?
> 
> The image ID comes from the config digest, and nothing promises that `podman import` will continue generate a config with predictable digests = the image ID of the outcome of `podman import` is unspecified and could, in principle, change from run to run.

I have a feeling we might be talking at cross purposes here. The `podman import` command does import a tar file and will make a new config and image ID. What I was talking about is `podman load` which loads an image in oci-archive format - in this case, the image ID comes from the hash of the config from the oci-archive as far as I can tell.

In my comment above, I should have written 'I'm not sure podman load should convert on import'

Thanks! In the case of `podman load` (and the 4 currently-supported formats), I feel comfortable in saying that sparse layers are invalid, and implementing conversion is unnecessary. It would still be nice to detect & reject such inputs.
fixed downstream, feel free to close if required.
